title: Causal Inference in Business & Economic Settings
abstract: An applied investigation into causal machine learning methods — Double Machine Learning, Generalized Random Forests, and the DoWhy framework — as tools for economic policy evaluation. Drawing on UofT's first delivery of the Causal ML course under Dr. Nazanin Khazra, this paper demonstrates how treatment effect estimation can move beyond correlation to support rigorous business decisions.

body.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: Causal inference has long been the province of academic economists and epidemiologists — fields where the difference between correlation and causation carries enormous consequence. In recent years, a new generation of tools has brought this rigour into business analytics and applied data science. This paper examines how methods from the causal ML literature can be deployed in a corporate setting.

The central question we address is deceptively simple: when a firm changes a policy, does the policy change cause the observed outcome, or is the observed outcome the result of confounding factors that happened to coincide with the policy change? Answering this question correctly is the difference between evidence-based management and expensive coincidence.

body.heading: Methodology
paragraphs: We draw primarily on three methodological pillars. First, Double Machine Learning (DML), introduced by Chernozhukov et al. (2018), which uses cross-fitting and regularised regression to partial out the influence of high-dimensional controls before estimating treatment effects. Second, Generalized Random Forests (GRF) for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation, allowing us to understand which sub-populations benefit most from a given policy. Third, the DoWhy causal graph framework, which forces explicit declaration of modelling assumptions before any estimation takes place.

Data was sourced from a combination of synthetic experiments and publicly available economic panel datasets. All estimation was performed in Python using the EconML, DoWhy, and GRF (via rpy2 bridge) libraries.

body.heading: Results & Discussion
paragraphs: Our core finding is that naive OLS estimates of treatment effects in the presence of selection bias are systematically overstated by a median factor of 1.4 across the scenarios we examined. DML estimates, by contrast, were within 8% of the ground-truth treatment effects in synthetic experiments designed to have known answers.

Heterogeneous treatment effect analysis revealed meaningful sub-group variation: policy effects that appeared null on average masked positive effects for one sub-group and negative effects for another. This finding has direct implications for targeted intervention design in business contexts.

The DoWhy framework proved its value not in the estimation phase but in the assumption-declaration phase: making the DAG explicit three times forced us to identify a previously overlooked backdoor path in our original specification, which would have biased estimates in the naive approach.

body.heading: Conclusion
paragraphs: Causal machine learning methods are now mature enough for routine deployment in business analytics. The key bottleneck is not computational — modern Python libraries handle the estimation efficiently — but conceptual: practitioners must develop the habit of drawing the causal graph before reaching for the regression. We hope this paper serves as a practical bridge between academic causal inference literature and applied economic data science.

title: Panel Data Methods in Economic Growth Analysis
abstract: A comparative study of fixed-effects, random-effects, and dynamic panel estimators applied to cross-country economic growth data. Using Arellano-Bond GMM and Blundell-Bond system GMM, this paper examines the persistence of growth shocks and the role of institutional quality in long-run convergence.

body.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: The empirics of economic growth have been shaped decisively by the availability of panel data — repeated observations of the same countries over time. Panel data allows researchers to control for unobserved country-level heterogeneity, separating genuine convergence dynamics from the spurious correlation introduced by omitted variables that happen to correlate with both initial income and subsequent growth.

This paper revisits the classic convergence debate with a modern econometric toolkit, comparing the performance of static and dynamic panel estimators on a balanced panel of 68 countries observed over four decades.

body.heading: Econometric Framework
paragraphs: We begin with the standard Mankiw-Romer-Weil specification, augmented with institutional quality measures from the World Governance Indicators database. The baseline fixed-effects estimator is consistent when T is large but suffers from the Nickell bias when T is small relative to N — precisely our situation with T = 10 five-year periods.

To address this, we implement the Arellano-Bond GMM estimator, which differences out fixed effects and uses lagged levels as instruments. We then apply the Blundell-Bond system GMM, which adds level equations with lagged differences as instruments, recovering efficiency when the autoregressive parameter is close to unity.

body.heading: Findings
paragraphs: Conditional convergence is confirmed across all specifications, with an estimated convergence rate of approximately 2.1% per year under system GMM — closely matching the theoretical prediction of the Solow model and prior empirical work. Institutional quality enters significantly, with a one standard deviation improvement in the rule-of-law index associated with a 0.6 percentage point increase in steady-state growth.

The Sargan-Hansen test for instrument validity is comfortably passed across specifications, lending credibility to our identification strategy. First-stage F-statistics for weak instruments are above 10 throughout.

title: Consumer Behaviour Analytics: A Machine Learning Approach
abstract: Applying gradient boosting, classification trees, and Shapley value decomposition to a retail dataset to identify drivers of customer churn and lifetime value. The paper demonstrates how interpretable ML methods can bridge the gap between predictive accuracy and actionable business insight.

body.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: Customer churn prediction is one of the oldest problems in applied machine learning, yet it remains perpetually relevant because the cost of customer acquisition routinely exceeds the cost of retention by an order of magnitude. This paper applies modern gradient boosting methods to a retail transaction dataset, with particular attention to model interpretability via Shapley value decomposition.

Our contribution is methodological as much as empirical: we demonstrate a workflow that moves from raw transaction data to actionable segmentation and targeted intervention recommendations, preserving interpretability at every step.

body.heading: Data & Feature Engineering
paragraphs: The dataset comprises 18 months of transaction records from a mid-size Canadian retailer, covering approximately 240,000 unique customers. Features were engineered using RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary Value) decomposition, supplemented with behavioural features including product category diversity, inter-purchase time variability, and promotional sensitivity.

Class imbalance — churn events constituted approximately 8% of the sample — was addressed via stratified sampling and class-weight adjustment rather than synthetic oversampling, to preserve the real distributional properties of the churn event.

body.heading: Model Results
paragraphs: LightGBM achieved an AUC-ROC of 0.847 on the held-out test set, compared to 0.791 for logistic regression baseline. More importantly, SHAP decomposition revealed that recency alone accounted for 41% of total feature importance — a finding with immediate operational consequence: the highest-leverage intervention is re-engagement within the first 45 days of inactivity.

Cluster analysis of SHAP interaction effects identified three distinct churn archetypes: price-sensitive defectors (responsive to discount offers), life-event churners (non-recoverable without product category extension), and disengaged loyalists (recoverable through personalised communication). Each archetype requires a different retention strategy.
